original_abstract:
The ongoing hybrid armed conflict involving Russia—characterized as the Special Military Operation—has presented the Armed Forces' medical services with an array of unprecedented challenges. This review synthesizes international literature indexed in PubMed® to evaluate the environmental and operational factors influencing military medical performance, focusing primarily on the frameworks of NATO member states. Analysis indicates that the primary constraints on operational effectiveness are the quantitative and qualitative staffing of medical units, the specialized training required to treat wartime pathologies, and the continuous maintenance of these high-stakes competencies.

Critical to the successful delivery of medical care is the preservation of facility integrity amidst targeted kinetic strikes, alongside the capacity to utilize infrastructure within hostile or newly occupied territories. At the prehospital level, mortality reduction is hampered by fatal hemorrhages and traumatic brain injuries resulting from mine-blast trauma. Consequently, the rapid application of tourniquets, expedited evacuation protocols, and emergency blood transfusions are the most significant factors in lowering prehospital fatality rates.

Among survivors, limb injuries represent the most substantial long-term challenge; these casualties impose a societal burden that exceeds the "years lived with disability" (YLD) associated with major non-communicable diseases, including oncology and cardiology. Statistically, modern conflicts are dominated by mine-blast injuries (61.4–83.5%) and head trauma (20.9–59.0%) in terms of lethality. However, limb injuries (45.7%) represent the absolute majority of the subsequent disease burden, necessitating a concentrated strategic focus for military medical services. Despite these shared challenges, a global consensus remains elusive regarding the optimal echelon and placement for the delivery of qualified and specialized medical care.
humanized_abstract:
The Armed Forces’ health care system of the Russian Federation has to deal with an unprecedented range of challenges resulting from the ongoing hybrid armed conflict with Russia—the Special Military Operation. This review article synthesizes the world literature indexed in PubMed® and provides an overview of the environmental and operational factors affecting the military medical capability within the context of medical and healthcare systems of the NATO member countries. Analysis of literature shows that main factors limiting operational capacity of the health care system of the Armed Forces are personnel strength and composition of health care units, necessity for specialized medical skills for care of war-related injuries, and continuous training of health care personnel for contingency of armed conflict.

Maintaining the integrity of a medical facility to deliver care before, during and after an attack is critical. In addition, being able to use the infrastructure in hostile or newly occupied areas is important. The reasons for successful prehospital care and mortality reduction include: Massive Hemorrhaging secondary to MBT causing death Traumatic Brain Injury secondary to MBT For the most prehospital interventions for fatality reduction include: The immediate application of a tourniquet to prevent continued hemorrhaging the implementation of a Medical Evacuation plan in a timely manner the administration of Blood/Blood Products to prevent further exsanguination and death.

Amputees and limb injured survivors deal with long term aftermath that, based on the “years lived with disability” (YLD) method to measure disability prevalence of selected major non-communicable conditions, such as cancer and cardiovascular diseases, may indeed constitute heavier and possibly more demanding burden on the society. A review of modern warfare casualties indicate mine-blast injuries account for between 61.4% and 83.5% and head trauma for between 20.9% and 59% of fatal casualties. Despite the higher casualty incidence of limb injuries which makes it a higher burden in absolute terms, military health services need to take concentrated steps towards strategy making that can be effective in dealing with the problem. Unfortunately, there is not as yet a consesus opinion in respect of the most appropriate level and location of medical care provided in this context.
